items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.title: Understanding Amenorrhea: Definitions and Evaluation Timelines
items.content: Amenorrhea is defined as the absence of menstrual periods, classified into primary (never occurred) or secondary (cessation for 3–6 months). Primary amenorrhea occurs in <1% of females. Evaluation timelines vary based on age, presence of secondary sexual characteristics, and cyclic pelvic pain.
keyPoints: Primary amenorrhea: Absence of menarche by age 16 or earlier if other factors are present (growth, secondary sexual characteristics, cyclic pelvic pain).
Secondary amenorrhea: Cessation for 3 months in any specific year; evaluation recommended after 6 months in women with previously irregular cycles.

Evaluation timelines: Initiate by age 15-16 with normal development; age 13 without secondary characteristics or if height < third percentile; age 12-13 with breast development and cyclic pelvic pain.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.title: Characteristics of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (AUB)
items.content: Abnormal uterine bleeding describes menstrual irregularities in frequency, cyclicity, duration, or volume outside pregnancy. AUB affects up to one-third of women between menarche and menopause.
keyPoints: Normal menstrual cycle: Every 21-35 days, lasting 4-7 days with up to 80 mL blood loss.
AUB diagnosis: Variations in frequency, cyclicity, duration, or volume.
Prevalence: Up to one-third of women between menarche and menopause experience AUB.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.title: Etiologies of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (PALM-COEIN)
items.content: The PALM-COEIN acronym categorizes AUB etiologies into structural and nonstructural causes. Structural causes include polyp, adenomyosis, leiomyoma, malignancy, and hyperplasia; nonstructural causes encompass coagulopathy, ovulatory dysfunction, endometrial issues, iatrogenic factors, and not yet classified.
keyPoints: PALM-COEIN acronym: Structural (polyp, adenomyosis, leiomyoma, malignancy/hyperplasia) and nonstructural causes (coagulopathy, ovulatory dysfunction, endometrial, iatrogenic, not yet classified).
Oligo- and anovulation often linked to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Pregnancy as the Primary Exclusion in Amenorrhea Evaluation
items.content: In evaluating amenorrhea, pregnancy must be excluded first regardless of patient history due to its high prevalence as a cause.
keyPoints: Pregnancy is the most common cause of amenorrhea and should always be ruled out initially.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Differential Diagnosis for Primary Amenorrhea with Normal Pubertal Development
items.content: In patients presenting with primary amenorrhea but normal pubertal development and a blind vagina, consider transverse vaginal septum, imperforate hymen, müllerian agenesis, or androgen insensitivity syndrome.
keyPoints: Obstruction by a transverse vaginal septum or imperforate hymen is possible.
Müllerian agenesis (Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser syndrome) can be caused by mutations in the WNT4 gene.
Androgen insensitivity syndrome accounts for ~10% of primary amenorrhea cases and involves a 46,XY karyotype with female external genitalia due to lack of androgen receptor responsiveness.
Absence of pubic and axillary hair helps distinguish AIS from müllerian agenesis.
Testosterone levels in AIS are typically in the male range.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Early Surgical Intervention for Outflow Tract Obstruction
items.content: Obstruction in the uterine outflow tract presents with dysmenorrhea and cyclic lower abdominal pain without menstruation. Prompt diagnosis and surgical intervention are crucial to prevent complications such as endometriosis due to retrograde menstrual flow.
keyPoints: Dysmenorrhea or lower abdominal cyclic pain with no menses indicates obstruction.
Ultrasound and MRI can accurately identify reproductive tract anomalies.
Surgery should be performed promptly after diagnosis to reduce the risk of endometriosis from retrograde menstrual flow.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.title: Management and Risks in Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS)
items.content: In AIS, gonadectomy is necessary due to the risk of gonadoblastoma. Estrogen replacement therapy is required post-gonadectomy, and vaginal dilatation may be needed for sexual function.
keyPoints: Gonadectomy is recommended in AIS due to gonadoblastoma risk.
Estrogen replacement therapy should follow gonadectomy.
Vaginal dilatation may be required post-gonadectomy.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Functional Hypothalamic Amenorrhea
items.content: Functional HA is diagnosed by exclusion, characterized by low gonadotropins and normal prolactin levels. It's associated with energy imbalance or stress, necessitating a thorough history and physical examination.
keyPoints: Functional HA diagnosis requires exclusion of other hypogonadotropic causes.
Characterized by low gonadotropins and normal prolactin levels.
Associated conditions include eating disorders, excessive exercise, and chronic diseases.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) in Premature Ovarian Failure
items.content: Primary ovarian insufficiency, formerly known as premature menopause or premature ovarian failure, is characterized by impaired ovarian function before age 40 and accounts for approximately 10% of secondary amenorrhea cases. It results from the loss of negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, leading to elevated FSH levels, which are a more reliable marker than LH due to their stability.
keyPoints: POI is defined as ovarian failure in women <40 years old, accounting for ~10% of secondary amenorrhea.
FSH is a better marker of ovarian failure compared to LH because its levels are less variable.

Further evaluation after POI diagnosis includes karyotype testing, serum anti-cortical and 21-hydroxylase antibodies, thyroid function tests, FMR1 premutation screening, and bone mineral density assessment.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.title: Evaluating Hypergonadotropic Hypogonadism Beyond POI
items.content: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism can occur in conditions other than primary ovarian insufficiency, such as mutations in FSH or LH receptors. Aromatase deficiency and 17α-hydroxylase deficiency present with decreased estrogen and elevated gonadotropins, leading to hyperandrogenism and hypertension, respectively.
keyPoints: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism can result from mutations in FSH or LH receptors.
Aromatase deficiency is associated with decreased estrogen, elevated gonadotropins, and hyperandrogenism.
17α-hydroxylase deficiency presents with decreased estrogen, elevated gonadotropins, and hypertension.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.title: Management of Bone Density in Functional Hypothalamic Amenorrhea
items.content: In cases of functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (HA) or anorexia nervosa, hormone replacement alone may not suffice for maintaining bone density. A multidisciplinary approach involving behavioral health professionals is crucial.
keyPoints: Hormone replacement alone may be insufficient for bone density in functional HA/anorexia nervosa
Multidisciplinary approach including behavioral health is essential
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.title: Fertility Treatment in Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism
items.content: For patients with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism seeking fertility, treatment involves both exogenous FSH and LH to stimulate ovulation.
keyPoints: Treatment for fertility requires exogenous FSH and LH in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of PCOS Using Rotterdam Criteria
items.content: PCOS diagnosis in adults requires two out of three criteria: irregular menses, hyperandrogenism, or polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. In adolescents, the diagnosis is based only on irregular menses and hyperandrogenism.
keyPoints: Rotterdam criteria require two of three features for PCOS diagnosis in adults
Criteria include: <8 menses/year, hyperandrogenism (Ferriman-Gallwey score >4–6), polycystic ovaries (≥20 antral follicles or volume ≥10 cm³)
Adolescent diagnosis relies on irregular menses and hyperandrogenism only
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.title: First-Line Treatment and Monitoring in PCOS
items.content: The initial treatment for women with PCOS not seeking pregnancy involves combined hormonal contraceptives to regulate menstrual cycles and reduce serum androgens. Regular screening for obesity, hypertension, glycemic control, depression, and anxiety is essential at diagnosis and subsequently at regular intervals.
keyPoints: Combined hormonal contraceptives are first-line treatment to regulate menstrual cycles and decrease serum androgens by increasing sex hormone-binding globulin levels.
Screening for obesity, hypertension, glycemic control, depression, and anxiety should occur at the time of diagnosis and then regularly.
Overweight and obese women require a fasting lipid profile at diagnosis.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.title: Management of Hyperandrogenism and Infertility in PCOS
items.content: If hormonal contraceptives are ineffective after 6 months for hyperandrogenic symptoms, antiandrogens like spironolactone or flutamide can be considered. Letrozole is the first-line treatment for ovulation induction, followed by clomiphene citrate with or without metformin.
keyPoints: Antiandrogens such as spironolactone and flutamide are options if hormonal contraceptives fail after 6 months for hyperandrogenic symptoms management.
Letrozine is the first-line treatment for ovulation induction, followed by clomiphene citrate with or without metformin.
Injectable gonadotropins can be used to induce monofollicular growth but increase the risk of hyperstimulation.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.title: Identifying Life-Threatening Conditions in Pelvic Pain
items.content: The first priority when evaluating pelvic pain is to identify life-threatening conditions that may require emergent surgical management. This includes assessing for shock and peritoneal signs.
keyPoints: Life-threatening conditions such as shock and peritoneal signs must be identified promptly.
Emergent surgical management may be required in these cases.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.title: Assessing Pregnancy Risk in Acute Pelvic Pain
items.content: In the evaluation of pelvic pain, it is crucial to identify the possibility of pregnancy early by obtaining a menstrual history and conducting $ \beta $-human chorionic gonadotropin ( $ \beta $-hCG) testing.
keyPoints: Menstrual history should be obtained to assess for potential pregnancy.
$ \beta $-hCG testing is essential to confirm or rule out pregnancy.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)
items.content: Pelvic inflammatory disease is diagnosed based on clinical symptoms and examination findings, with treatment initiated presumptively even without positive test results. Risk factors include age <25 years, multiple sexual partners, STIs, or recent uterine procedures.
keyPoints: Common presentation: bilateral lower abdominal pain exacerbated by sexual activity.
Risk factors: age <25 years, multiple sexual partners, history of STIs, recent uterine procedures.
Clinical findings: cervical motion tenderness, adnexal pain, vaginal discharge.
Diagnostic aids: wet mount preparation, nucleic acid amplification tests for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
Treatment can be initiated based on clinical diagnosis without positive test results.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Clinical Approach to Ectopic Pregnancy
items.content: Ectopic pregnancy, occurring in 1–2% of pregnancies, presents with acute lower abdominal pain and requires high suspicion. Diagnosis involves assessing menstrual history, measuring $ \beta $-hCG levels, and performing pelvic ultrasound.
keyPoints: Incidence: 1–2% of all pregnancies.
Common site: fallopian tubes.
Symptoms: acute lower abdominal pain, hemodynamic instability, peritoneal signs.
$ \beta $-hCG doubling time: every 48 hours in early first trimester.
Discriminatory zone for ultrasound: 1500–3000 IU/mL.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Differentiating Ovarian Torsion from Other Adnexal Pathologies
items.content: Ovarian torsion presents with acute, unilateral pain and requires prompt diagnosis via Doppler ultrasound to assess blood flow. It is a diagnosis of exclusion unless absent blood flow is demonstrated.
keyPoints: Presentation: acute onset of unilateral, intermittent pain.
Diagnosis: Doppler ultrasound showing absent blood flow to the ovary.
Differential diagnoses include rupture or bleeding of ovarian cysts.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Management of Acute Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)
items.content: Immediate treatment of PID is crucial upon diagnosis to prevent long-term complications such as ectopic pregnancy and infertility. Outpatient management includes 250 mg IM ceftriaxone and a 14-day course of oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily.
keyPoints: Immediate treatment of PID is indicated even if the diagnosis is presumed or symptoms are mild.
Outpatient treatment: 250 mg IM ceftriaxone + 14 days of oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily.
Inpatient therapy recommended for acute presentations with high fever, nausea, vomiting, severe abdominal pain, or tubo-ovarian abscess.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Treatment of Ectopic Pregnancy
items.content: Surgical treatment is necessary for ectopic pregnancies presenting with acute pain, hemodynamic instability, or signs of intraperitoneal bleeding. Methotrexate can be used in clinically stable women with unruptured ectopic pregnancies and is effective in ~90% of cases.
keyPoints: Surgery required for ectopic pregnancy with acute symptoms or hemodynamic instability.
Methotrexate treatment is an option for stable patients with unruptured ectopic pregnancies, effective in ~90% of cases.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Endometriosis
items.content: Endometriosis is diagnosed through pelvic ultrasound, MRI, or diagnostic laparoscopy. It can cause dysmenorrhea, painful intercourse, and bowel movements. Treatment may involve ablation or excision of lesions.
keyPoints: Pelvic ultrasound and MRI are used in the initial workup; laparoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis.
Endometriosis causes cyclic or continuous pain, not always correlating with severity.
Treatment involves lesion ablation or excision.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.title: Effective Management of Dysmenorrhea with NSAIDs and Hormonal Contraceptives
items.content: NSAIDs are highly effective for dysmenorrhea, providing over 80% sustained response rates. They should be initiated before menses onset and continued for at least 2-3 days. Combined or progestin-only hormonal contraceptives also effectively reduce symptoms.
keyPoints: NSAIDs provide >80% sustained response rates in dysmenorrhea treatment.
Ibuprofen, naproxen, ketoprofen, mefenamic acid, and nimesulide are superior to placebo.
Treatment should start before menses onset and continue for 2-3 days.
Combined or progestin-only hormonal contraceptives effectively reduce dysmenorrhea symptoms.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.title: Management Strategies for Endometriosis and Fibroids
items.content: Endometriosis can be managed with combined hormonal contraceptives or continuous progestin, and endometriomas may not require surgical removal unless symptomatic. Fibroids management depends on their number and location, with options including medical treatments like NSAIDs and GnRH agonists, or surgical interventions such as myomectomy or hysterectomy.
keyPoints: Endometriosis can be treated with combined hormonal contraceptives or continuous progestin.
Endometriomas on ultrasound do not require surgery unless symptomatic.
Fibroids management includes medical (NSAIDs, GnRH agonists) and surgical options (myomectomy, hysterectomy).
FDA approved elagolix with estradiol/norethindrone acetate and relugolix with estradiol/norethindrone acetate for fibroids.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.question: What is the traditional age definition for primary amenorrhea?
items.explanation: Primary amenorrhea is traditionally defined as the absence of menarche by age 16.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.question: Which acronym describes the etiologies for abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB)?
items.explanation: The acronym PALM-COEIN is used to describe the etiologies for AUB.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.question: What percentage of women experience abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) between menarche and menopause?
items.explanation: Up to one-third of women experience AUB symptoms between menarche and menopause.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.question: What is the typical menstrual cycle length for a woman who is ovulating consistently?
items.explanation: For a woman who is ovulating consistently, the typical menstrual cycle length is about 28 days.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which condition is associated with a 46,XY karyotype but presents with female external genitalia due to lack of androgen receptor responsiveness?
items.explanation: Androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS) is an X-linked recessive disorder where individuals have a 46,XY karyotype but present with female external genitalia due to androgen receptor unresponsiveness.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: What accounts for more than 90% of cases of Asherman's syndrome?
options: Obstruction by a transverse vaginal septum
items.explanation: Asherman's syndrome is primarily caused by curettage performed after pregnancy complications, accounting for more than 90% of cases.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which condition can be distinguished from müllerian agenesis by the absence of pubic and axillary hair?
items.explanation: Patients with Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) lack pubic and axillary hair, distinguishing them from those with müllerian agenesis.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.question: Which imaging modality is more accurate for identifying reproductive tract anomalies before surgery in patients with outflow tract obstruction?
items.explanation: An MRI scan can more accurately identify the reproductive tract anomaly prior to surgery.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.question: What is indicated after gonadectomy in patients with AIS?
items.explanation: Estrogen replacement is indicated after gonadectomy in AIS.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.question: Which condition may require surgical intervention to allow sexual intercourse, although vaginal dilatation might be adequate for some patients?
items.explanation: Müllerian agenesis may require surgical intervention to allow sexual intercourse.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.question: What is a common feature associated with Kallmann's syndrome in women?
items.explanation: IHH is associated with anosmia in half of the women, termed Kallmann's syndrome.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.question: Which marker is considered a better indicator of ovarian failure in primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) due to its stability and loss of negative feedback effects?
items.explanation: FSH is a better marker because it is less variable than LH and reflects loss of negative feedback from both estradiol and inhibins.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.question: What should be offered to women of reproductive age prior to gonadotoxic chemotherapy or pelvic radiation treatment?
items.explanation: New approaches like ovarian, oocyte, and embryo cryopreservation should be offered to women of reproductive age prior to gonadotoxic treatments.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.question: In primary ovarian insufficiency (POI), what is a recommended follow-up test due to its association with increased risk of severe intellectual disability in male children?
items.explanation: FMR1 premutation screening is important due to the association with fragile X syndrome and risk of intellectual disability in male children.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.question: Which hormone replacement regimen is recommended for women with amenorrhea until the usual age of menopause?
options: Low-dose estrogen/progesterone regimens or oral contraceptive pills
items.explanation: Hormone replacement with either low-dose estrogen/progesterone regimens or oral contraceptive pills is recommended for bone and cardiovascular protection.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.question: What additional approach may be necessary in women with functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (HA) or anorexia nervosa to maintain bone density?
options: Long-term multidisciplinary approach including behavioral health professionals
items.explanation: A more long-term multidisciplinary approach including behavioral health professionals is essential for these women.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.question: Which treatment is required for patients with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism who wish to achieve fertility?
items.explanation: Patients with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism interested in fertility require treatment with both exogenous FSH and LH.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.question: What is a limitation for the use of oocyte donation in women with Turner's syndrome?
items.explanation: The use of oocyte donation is limited by increased cardiovascular risk in pregnancy for women with Turner's syndrome.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.question: What is the first-line treatment for women with PCOS who are not attempting to become pregnant?
options: Antiandrogens such as spironolactone and flutamide
items.explanation: Combined hormonal contraceptives are used to regulate menstrual cycles and decrease serum androgens.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.question: What should be considered if there is an inadequate response to hormonal contraceptives after 6 months for managing hyperandrogenic symptoms in PCOS?
options: Increase the dose of estrogen in hormonal contraceptives
Switch to a continuous regimen of hormonal contraceptives
Consider antiandrogens such as spironolactone and flutamide
items.explanation: Antiandrogens like spironolactone and flutamide can be considered if hormonal contraceptives are inadequate.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.question: Which medication is recommended as the first-line treatment for ovulation induction in women with PCOS?
items.explanation: Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor, is the first-line treatment for ovulation induction.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.question: Which of the following mechanisms can cause pelvic pain?
items.explanation: Pelvic pain can be caused by inflammation of the parietal peritoneum, among other mechanisms.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.question: In up to what percentage of cases can pelvic pain be attributed to gastrointestinal problems?
items.explanation: Pelvic pain can be attributed to gastrointestinal problems in up to 60% of cases.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.question: What should be identified as soon as possible when evaluating pelvic pain?
items.explanation: The possibility of pregnancy should be identified early using menstrual history and β-hCG testing.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.question: Which symptom is most suggestive of perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome) in a patient with pelvic inflammatory disease?
items.explanation: Right upper quadrant pain is suggestive of perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome) in the context of PID.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.question: What is a common finding on pelvic examination for patients with pelvic inflammatory disease?
items.explanation: Cervical motion tenderness is a common finding on pelvic examination in PID.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.question: Which of the following tests can aid in diagnosing pelvic inflammatory disease?
options: Wet mount preparation and nucleic acid amplification tests for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae
items.explanation: A wet mount preparation and nucleic acid amplification tests for C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae can aid in diagnosing PID.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.question: What is the recommended frequency of testing for Chlamydia trachomatis in sexually active women under 25 years old?
items.explanation: Annual testing for C. trachomatis is recommended in sexually active women under 25 years old.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for PID in patients eligible for outpatient management?
options: 250 mg IM ceftriaxone and a 14-day course of oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily
items.explanation: Outpatient treatment for PID includes 250 mg IM ceftriaxone and a 14-day course of oral doxycycline.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.question: Which condition is treated with methotrexate in clinically stable women presenting with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy?
items.explanation: Methotrexate is used for treatment of unruptured ectopic pregnancies in clinically stable women.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.question: For which condition is conservative management important to avoid unnecessary surgery and associated risks?
items.explanation: Conservative management of ovarian cysts is crucial if torsion is not suspected to avoid unnecessary surgery.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.question: What is the primary mechanism thought to cause mittelschmerz or ovulation pain?
options: Rapid expansion of the dominant follicle
Peritoneal irritation by follicular fluid released at the time of ovulation
items.explanation: Mittelschmerz is thought to be caused by both rapid expansion of the dominant follicle and peritoneal irritation.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.question: Which of the following treatments for dysmenorrhea has been shown to provide >80% sustained response rates?
items.explanation: NSAIDs are very effective and provide >80% sustained response rates for dysmenorrhea.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.question: What is the recommended timing for initiating treatment with NSAIDs to achieve the best response in managing dysmenorrhea?
options: Prior to the onset of menses
items.explanation: For best response, NSAID treatment should be initiated prior to the onset of menses.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.question: Which medication was withdrawn in Europe and Canada for the management of fibroids?
items.explanation: The selective progesterone receptor modulator ulipristal acetate was withdrawn in Europe and Canada for fibroids.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.statement: Race or ethnicity influences the prevalence of amenorrhea.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that race or ethnicity influences the prevalence of amenorrhea.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.statement: Pregnancy should be excluded early in any evaluation of menstrual irregularity.
items.explanation: Pregnancy should indeed be excluded early when evaluating for menstrual irregularities.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Pregnancy is the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea.
items.explanation: Pregnancy must be excluded as a cause of all amenorrhea, but it is not specifically stated as the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Müllerian agenesis can result from mutations in the WNT4 gene.
items.explanation: The passage states that Müllerian agenesis (Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser syndrome) can be caused by mutations in the WNT4 gene.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.statement: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea is primarily associated with hyperprolactinemia.
items.explanation: Functional HA is a diagnosis of exclusion and typically presents with normal prolactin levels.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.statement: Sheehan's syndrome can cause amenorrhea in the postpartum period.
items.explanation: Amenorrhea may be caused by pituitary necrosis, known as Sheehan's syndrome.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.statement: Ovarian biopsy is indicated for diagnosing primary ovarian insufficiency.
items.explanation: Ovarian biopsy is not indicated in the diagnosis of POI.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)
items.statement: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism can occur due to mutations in FSH or LH receptors.
items.explanation: Mutations in FSH or LH receptors are rare causes of hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 2)

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.statement: PCOS diagnosis requires the presence of all three Rotterdam criteria.
items.explanation: The diagnosis of PCOS is confirmed by the presence of two out of the three Rotterdam criteria.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.statement: Lean oligo-ovulatory patients with PCOS generally have high LH levels.
items.explanation: These patients typically exhibit high LH levels in the presence of normal to low FSH and estradiol.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.statement: Metformin alone is recommended for endometrial protection in women with PCOS.
items.explanation: Metformin alone is not recommended for endometrial protection.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
items.statement: Women with PCOS are at increased risk of early miscarriage and gestational diabetes.
items.explanation: PCOS increases the risk of early miscarriage, gestational diabetes, among other complications.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.statement: Pelvic pain may reflect extrapelvic disorders that refer pain to the pelvis.
items.explanation: Pelvic pain can indeed be referred from extrapelvic disorders.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain
items.statement: Urinary tract and musculoskeletal disorders are rare causes of pelvic pain.
items.explanation: Urinary tract and musculoskeletal disorders are common causes of pelvic pain.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · PELVIC PAIN | Pelvic Pain

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.statement: A presumptive clinical diagnosis of PID can be sufficient to prescribe treatment even if test results are negative.
items.explanation: Treatment for PID can be initiated based on a presumptive clinical diagnosis without positive test results.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.statement: Ectopic pregnancy is the most common cause of acute pelvic pain.
items.explanation: While ectopic pregnancy can present with acute pelvic pain, it is not the most common cause.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.statement: The CA-125 level has high negative predictive value for diagnosing endometriosis.
items.explanation: CA-125 level has low negative predictive value in the diagnosis of endometriosis.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.statement: Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by underlying pelvic pathology.
items.explanation: Primary dysmenorrhea results from hormone-dependent prostaglandin pathway mechanisms, not underlying pelvic pathology.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.statement: Endometriomas on ultrasound imaging always require surgical removal.
items.explanation: Endometriomas can be medically managed unless they increase in size or there is persistent pain.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS
items.statement: Chronic pain and dysmenorrhea associated with fibroids can only be managed surgically.
items.explanation: Fibroid-associated chronic pain and dysmenorrhea can be managed medically or surgically.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · DYSMENORRHEA | ENDOMETRIOSIS | FIBROIDS

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.assertion: Primary amenorrhea is a rare disorder.
items.reason: It occurs in less than 1% of the female population.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why primary amenorrhea is considered rare.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE
items.assertion: Recent guidelines recommend routine pelvic examination for all women.
items.reason: Pelvic examination is important for evaluating amenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and pelvic pain.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because recent guidelines do not recommend routine pelvic examinations in asymptomatic, average-risk women other than for cervical cancer screening.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain | DEFINITION AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.assertion: Gonadectomy is required in AIS due to the risk of gonadoblastoma.
items.reason: AIS involves dysgenetic gonads which have a risk of developing into gonadoblastoma.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)
items.assertion: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea can be diagnosed with high prolactin levels.
items.reason: Functional HA is associated with normal prolactin levels.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; functional HA typically has normal prolactin levels.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Disorders of the Uterus or Outflow Tract (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.assertion: PCOS is associated with an increased risk of metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: The prevalence of obesity in PCOS significantly increases the risk of comorbidities including metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea
items.assertion: PCOS diagnosis in adolescents is based on all Rotterdam criteria.
items.reason: The ultrasound and AMH criteria are not established for this age group, so the diagnosis relies only on irregular menses and hyperandrogenism.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because PCOS diagnosis in adolescents does not use all Rotterdam criteria.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Hypo- and Hypergonadotropic Causes of Amenorrhea

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.assertion: Ovarian torsion typically presents as acute onset of unilateral, intermittent pain.
items.reason: Ovarian torsion is a diagnosis of exclusion unless absent blood flow to the ovary is demonstrated via Doppler ultrasound imaging.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why ovarian torsion presents with unilateral pain.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: ACUTE PELVIC PAIN
items.assertion: The incidence of ectopic pregnancy rupture is higher in developing countries.
items.reason: Access to care affects the incidence of fallopian tube rupture, which is ~18% in developed countries.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · ACUTE PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.assertion: Endometriosis can be diagnosed with transvaginal pelvic ultrasound.
items.reason: Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound may detect an endometrioma within the ovary.
items.explanation: While transvaginal ultrasound can detect endometriomas, it is not definitive for diagnosing all forms of endometriosis.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN

items.subtopic: Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
items.assertion: Pelvic MRI has higher sensitivity and specificity for the diagnosis of endometriosis than CA-125 levels.
items.reason: CA-125 level is increased in some cases of endometriosis but has low negative predictive value.
items.explanation: MRI provides better diagnostic accuracy compared to CA-125, which lacks specificity and sensitivity.
items.reference: Menstrual Disorders and Pelvic Pain · Acute Pelvic Pain | CHRONIC PELVIC PAIN
